branchingScenario.title: PBL: Ventricular System & Special sensory pathways — Clinical Reasoning

startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 50-year-old woman presents with headaches and visual field defects. Apply your knowledge of the ventricular system, CSF circulation, and visual pathways to guide diagnosis.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly applied knowledge of the ventricular system, CSF flow, and visual pathway anatomy to diagnose and manage this case.
You showed some knowledge but missed key concepts. Review the ventricular connections, types of hydrocephalus, and optic chiasm anatomy.
Review the fundamentals: ventricular system anatomy, CSF circulation, optic chiasm fibre crossing, and visual field defect patterns.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 50-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of progressive headaches (worse in the morning), nausea, and gradual loss of peripheral vision in both eyes. She has also noticed irregular menstrual cycles and mild galactorrhoea.

Examination findings:

  • Visual acuity: 6/6 bilaterally (central vision preserved)
  • Visual fields: Loss of temporal visual fields bilaterally
  • Fundoscopy: Early papilloedema
  • No other cranial nerve deficits

Key anatomy: The ventricular system includes the lateral ventricles (connected to the 3rd ventricle via the foramen of Monro), the 3rd ventricle (connected to the 4th via the cerebral aqueduct of Sylvius), and the 4th ventricle.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient has bilateral temporal visual field loss (bitemporal hemianopia). Which structure is being compressed, and which fibres are damaged?

alternatives.text: Optic chiasm — the nasal retinal fibres that cross at the chiasm are damaged, causing loss of temporal visual fields bilaterally

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: At the optic chiasm, nasal retinal fibres (which carry temporal visual field information) cross to the opposite side, while temporal retinal fibres remain ipsilateral. A pituitary tumour compressing the chiasm from below damages the crossing nasal fibres, causing bitemporal hemianopia — the classic visual field defect of chiasmal compression.

alternatives.text: Both optic nerves — bilateral optic neuritis causing peripheral vision loss

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Bilateral optic nerve damage would typically cause bilateral central scotomas or diffuse visual loss, not a clean bitemporal hemianopia. The pattern of bilateral temporal field loss specifically localises to the optic chiasm, where the crossing nasal retinal fibres are selectively damaged by midline compression from below.

alternatives.text: Lateral geniculate nucleus — relay station damage causing bilateral field cuts

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN) is a relay in the thalamus. Unilateral LGN damage would cause contralateral homonymous hemianopia, not bitemporal hemianopia. Bitemporal hemianopia specifically indicates optic chiasm compression, where crossing nasal retinal fibres are damaged.

params.text:
MRI Findings and Hydrocephalus

MRI reveals a pituitary macroadenoma (3 cm) extending superiorly to compress the optic chiasm. The tumour also partially obstructs the foramen of Monro.

Imaging shows:

  • Dilated lateral ventricles bilaterally
  • Mildly dilated 3rd ventricle
  • Normal-sized 4th ventricle

This pattern indicates obstructive (non-communicating) hydrocephalus — CSF flow is blocked at the foramen of Monro. Compare this with aqueductal stenosis, where the block is at the cerebral aqueduct (lateral and 3rd ventricles dilated, 4th ventricle normal).

branchingQuestion.question: In aqueductal stenosis (blockage of the cerebral aqueduct of Sylvius), which ventricles would be dilated?

alternatives.text: The lateral ventricles and 3rd ventricle would be dilated, while the 4th ventricle remains normal in size

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: The cerebral aqueduct connects the 3rd ventricle to the 4th ventricle. Obstruction here causes CSF to accumulate upstream — dilating the lateral ventricles and 3rd ventricle. The 4th ventricle, being downstream of the block, remains normal. This is the most common cause of congenital hydrocephalus.

alternatives.text: Only the 4th ventricle would be dilated, as CSF backs up below the blockage

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: CSF flows from the lateral ventricles → 3rd ventricle → cerebral aqueduct → 4th ventricle. A block at the aqueduct causes upstream dilation (lateral + 3rd ventricles), not downstream. The 4th ventricle would be normal or small. Think of it like a dam — water backs up upstream of the blockage.

alternatives.text: All four ventricles would be equally dilated because CSF circulates in a closed loop

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: CSF does not circulate in a closed loop — it flows in one direction: lateral ventricles → foramen of Monro → 3rd ventricle → cerebral aqueduct → 4th ventricle → foramina of Luschka and Magendie → subarachnoid space → arachnoid granulations. A block at any point causes dilation only upstream of the obstruction.

params.text:
Key Learning Points

  • Ventricular connections: Lateral ventricles → foramen of Monro → 3rd ventricle → cerebral aqueduct (of Sylvius) → 4th ventricle
  • Aqueductal stenosis: Dilated lateral and 3rd ventricles with normal 4th ventricle — most common cause of congenital hydrocephalus
  • Optic chiasm: Nasal retinal fibres cross; temporal retinal fibres remain ipsilateral
  • Bitemporal hemianopia: Classic sign of chiasmal compression (e.g., pituitary tumour) — damage to crossing nasal fibres
  • Pituitary tumour effects: Visual field defects (chiasm), hormonal disturbance (prolactin, GH, ACTH), and potentially hydrocephalus (foramen of Monro compression)
  • CSF flow: Produced by choroid plexus → ventricles → subarachnoid space → absorbed by arachnoid granulations into venous sinuses
